curated_content.name: Azure Architecture Patterns and Best Practices
Pluralsight Professional Training
curated_content.source: 37 courses on Azure architecture, microservices, data architecture, enterprise architecture

expressroute_vs_vpn.statement: ExpressRoute provides 99.95% SLA, up to 100 Gbps bandwidth, predictable latency, and private connectivity not traversing public internet. VPN Gateway provides 99.9% SLA (Basic/VpnGw1-5) or 99.95% (VpnGw1AZ-5AZ), up to 10 Gbps bandwidth, variable latency over public internet

expressroute_vs_vpn.title: ExpressRoute vs VPN Gateway for Hybrid Connectivity
expressroute_vs_vpn.description: Choose hybrid connectivity solution based on workload criticality, latency requirements, and bandwidth needs
expressroute_vs_vpn.rationale: ExpressRoute provides predictable latency, higher bandwidth, and better security through private connectivity. While VPN is suitable for dev/test and branch offices, production mission-critical workloads benefit significantly from ExpressRoute's characteristics
expressroute_vs_vpn.source: Azure Well-Architected Framework - Reliability and Performance

options.name: ExpressRoute (Primary)
options.reason: Best for production - predictable latency, high bandwidth, private connectivity

cons: Higher monthly cost ($50-$5,000+/month depending on bandwidth)
Longer setup time (weeks to months with ISP)

options.name: VPN Gateway
options.reason: Acceptable for dev/test, branch offices, or backup connectivity

cons: Lower bandwidth (up to 10 Gbps, typically much less)
More troubleshooting due to internet variability

options.name: ExpressRoute + VPN (HA)
options.reason: Best for critical workloads requiring highest availability

pros: Cost-effective backup (VPN cheaper than dual ExpressRoute)
cons: Application must handle path changes gracefully

decision_factors: Production + mission-critical → ExpressRoute (primary)
Need highest availability → ExpressRoute + VPN backup
Dev/test or branch offices → VPN Gateway alone
Latency-sensitive (< 10ms required) → ExpressRoute mandatory
Bandwidth > 1 Gbps → ExpressRoute strongly recommended
Cost-constrained + non-critical → VPN Gateway

azure_firewall_sizing.title: Azure Firewall for Hub-Spoke Topology
azure_firewall_sizing.description: Decide when Azure Firewall complexity and cost are justified vs simpler NSG-based approaches
azure_firewall_sizing.rationale: Azure Firewall Standard costs ~$1.25/hour (~$900/month) plus data processing. For small deployments, this can represent 20-50% of total Azure spend. NSGs provide stateful packet filtering at no cost. Application Gateway WAF or Front Door provide Layer 7 protection for web apps. The operational complexity of managing Azure Firewall rules is not justified for smaller environments
azure_firewall_sizing.source: Azure Well-Architected Framework - Cost Optimization + Pluralsight Training

when_not_applicable: Azure Firewall already deployed and working

options.name: Azure Firewall (Hub-Spoke)
options.reason: Best for large enterprises with complex network requirements

pros: Application and network rules in single pane
cons: Potential single point of failure (if not HA)

options.name: NSG-Based Segmentation
options.reason: Best for smaller deployments - cost-effective and simpler

pros: Applied at subnet or NIC level

options.name: Application Gateway WAF / Front Door
options.reason: Best for web application protection - Layer 7 security

pros: Lower cost than Azure Firewall for web apps

context_requirements: Can be used alongside NSGs or Firewall

decision_factors: Large enterprise (>100 VMs) + complex networks → Azure Firewall
Small deployment (<100 VMs) + simple needs → NSGs only
Web applications → Application Gateway WAF or Front Door WAF
Need FQDN filtering + centralized egress → Azure Firewall
Cost-constrained + no FQDN requirements → NSGs + App Gateway WAF
Consider Firewall when managing 5+ separate workloads with complex inter-communication

microservices_adoption.title: When to Adopt Microservices Architecture
microservices_adoption.description: Decide when microservices complexity is justified vs simpler architectures
microservices_adoption.rationale: Microservices introduce substantial complexity: distributed system challenges, eventual consistency, complex testing, increased operational overhead, and require high DevOps maturity. Many organizations adopt microservices prematurely without these capabilities, leading to 'distributed monoliths' with all downsides and none of the benefits
microservices_adoption.source: Pluralsight Microservices Training + Azure Architecture Center

when_not_applicable: Microservices already in production and working

options.name: Start with Simpler Architecture (N-tier, Web-Queue-Worker)
options.reason: Best default for most applications - provides benefits with far less complexity

pros: Can evolve to microservices later if needed
cons: May need refactoring if grows very large

context_requirements: Teams new to cloud or distributed systems

options.name: Adopt Microservices
options.reason: Only when you have genuine need and capability

pros: Fault isolation (one service failure doesn't crash all)
cons: Distributed system complexity (network calls, retries, timeouts)

context_requirements: Large application with proven scaling issues
Willingness to manage distributed system complexity

decision_factors: Default → Start with simpler architecture (monolith or N-tier)
Only adopt microservices when you have PROVEN need (not theoretical)
Adopt when: large team, independent scaling proven needed, rapid releases required, DevOps maturity high
Avoid when: small team, new to distributed systems, cost-constrained, rapid initial development needed
Can always refactor later - premature microservices is worse than starting simple

container_apps_vs_aks.title: Azure Container Apps vs AKS for Containerized Applications
container_apps_vs_aks.description: Choose between managed serverless containers (Container Apps) vs full Kubernetes (AKS)
container_apps_vs_aks.rationale: AKS provides full Kubernetes features but requires significant expertise to operate securely and reliably: node management, upgrade coordination, security patching, network policy configuration, service mesh setup. Container Apps provides serverless containers with KEDA autoscaling, Dapr integration, and managed ingress, eliminating most operational complexity while still supporting containerized apps. For 80% of containerized workloads, Container Apps operational overhead savings far outweigh flexibility loss
container_apps_vs_aks.source: Azure Container Apps Documentation + Pluralsight Training

options.name: Azure Container Apps
options.reason: Default choice for most containerized workloads - serverless with low ops overhead

pros: Serverless (pay per use, scale to zero)

options.name: Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS)
options.reason: Use when need full Kubernetes control or advanced features

cons: High operational overhead (cluster management, upgrades, patching)

decision_factors: Default → Container Apps (80% of use cases)
Use AKS when: need service mesh, Windows containers, advanced K8s features, existing K8s team
Start with Container Apps, migrate to AKS only if you hit limitations
Operational capacity: low → Container Apps, high → AKS
Team expertise: new to containers → Container Apps, Kubernetes experts → AKS

cosmos_db_evaluation.title: When to Use Cosmos DB vs Azure SQL Database
cosmos_db_evaluation.description: Choose database based on actual workload requirements, not aspirational global scale
cosmos_db_evaluation.rationale: Cosmos DB is powerful for global distribution and multi-model capabilities, but most applications don't need them and end up paying significant cost premium. Cosmos DB pricing at ~$24/month per 100 RU/s is expensive compared to SQL Database starting at ~$5/month. Cosmos DB is optimized for specific scenarios: global distribution with low latency worldwide, flexible schema changes, massive scale (>100 million ops/day)

when_not_applicable: Cosmos DB already in production with proper use case

options.name: Azure SQL Database
options.reason: Best default for most CRUD applications - cost-effective and feature-rich

pros: Azure Hybrid Benefit eligible (save up to 55%)
cons: Vertical scaling (limited to max SKU size)

options.name: Cosmos DB
options.reason: Use when you have specific global distribution or massive scale needs

pros: Multiple APIs (SQL, MongoDB, Cassandra, Gremlin, Table)
cons: Eventual consistency by default (strong costs more)

context_requirements: Global application requiring < 10ms latency in multiple regions
Truly flexible schema requirements (not just 'might change someday')

decision_factors: Default → Azure SQL Database (for most apps)
Use Cosmos DB when: global < 10ms latency needed, truly flexible schema, massive scale, IoT/telemetry
Don't use Cosmos DB for: regional apps, predictable schemas, < 1M requests/day, strong consistency without compromise, limited budget
Cost comparison: SQL DB can be 5-10x cheaper for similar workloads without global distribution needs

functions_cold_start.title: Azure Functions Consumption vs Premium Plan
functions_cold_start.description: Choose Functions plan based on latency requirements and traffic patterns
functions_cold_start.rationale: Consumption plan cold starts can take 3-10+ seconds depending on language and dependencies. While warm instances respond in milliseconds, the unpredictability of cold starts creates poor user experience. Premium plan eliminates cold starts with pre-warmed instances. Container Apps with minimum replica configuration also avoids cold starts

when_not_applicable: Not using Azure Functions (using App Service or Container Apps)

options.name: Consumption Plan
options.reason: Best for background processing, async workloads, cost optimization

pros: Auto-scale to zero (no cost when idle)

options.name: Premium Plan
options.reason: Best for user-facing APIs and consistent performance needs

cons: More expensive than App Service for always-on workloads

context_requirements: Latency must be sub-second and predictable

options.name: Dedicated (App Service Plan)
options.reason: Use when Functions run continuously and predictable hourly cost preferred

pros: Existing App Service Plan can be reused
cons: Less cost-effective than Consumption for intermittent workloads

context_requirements: Already have App Service Plan with capacity
VNet integration needed but Premium too expensive

decision_factors: Background/async processing → Consumption Plan
User-facing APIs → Premium Plan or Container Apps
Consistent performance required → Premium Plan
Cost-optimized + latency not critical → Consumption Plan
Always-on workloads + predictable cost → Dedicated (App Service Plan)
VNet integration needed → Premium or Dedicated

database_technology_selection.title: Choosing Between SQL Server and PostgreSQL in Azure
database_technology_selection.description: Select appropriate database technology based on licensing, features, expertise, and cost considerations
database_technology_selection.rationale: SQL Server offers Microsoft ecosystem integration and AHUB savings, while PostgreSQL provides open-source flexibility and lower TCO. Choice depends on existing investment, licensing, team expertise, and application requirements.
database_technology_selection.source: Microsoft Azure Database Comparison Documentation and Cost Analysis

when_applicable: New application development requiring relational database
when_not_applicable: Pure key-value storage (use Redis, Table Storage)
Existing database platform mandated by organization
Third-party application with specific database requirement

options.name: SQL Server (Azure SQL Database or Managed Instance)
options.reason: Best for existing SQL Server applications and Microsoft ecosystem integration

pros: Near 100% compatibility with on-premises SQL Server (Managed Instance)
Enterprise BI tools (Power BI, SSRS, SSAS) native support
Azure Hybrid Benefit saves up to 55% for customers with SA
SQL Server-specific features (CLR, Service Broker, Always Encrypted)
Familiar T-SQL for existing SQL Server teams
cons: Higher cost without Azure Hybrid Benefit ($2,400/month vs $1,200/month for 8vCore HA)
Some advanced SQL features require Business Critical tier
Cross-database queries only in Managed Instance (not SQL Database)

context_requirements: Existing SQL Server applications or expertise
Microsoft ecosystem (.NET, Azure AD, Power BI)
SQL Server licenses with Software Assurance (for AHUB)
Need SQL Server-specific features (CLR, Service Broker)

options.name: PostgreSQL (Flexible Server or Hyperscale)
options.reason: Best for new applications, open-source preference, and cost optimization

pros: 30-50% lower cost than SQL Server without AHUB ($1,200 vs $2,400/month for 8vCore HA)
Advanced data types (JSONB, arrays, hstore, custom types)
Hyperscale (Citus) for massive scale and multi-tenant SaaS
cons: Team learning curve if coming from SQL Server
No native .NET tools like SSRS/SSAS (use alternatives)
Less mature Azure integration than SQL Server
No superuser access in managed service
Migration from SQL Server requires code conversion

context_requirements: Need advanced data types (JSONB) or extensions (PostGIS)

options.name: MySQL (Flexible Server)
options.reason: Good for LAMP stack and existing MySQL applications

pros: Excellent for LAMP/LEMP stack (PHP, Python web apps)
Simpler to learn and administer than PostgreSQL or SQL Server
cons: Weaker SQL standards compliance than PostgreSQL
Limited advanced features compared to PostgreSQL
Limited JSON support compared to PostgreSQL JSONB

decision_factors: Existing investment (SQL Server apps/licenses → SQL Server, new apps → PostgreSQL)
Licensing (Have SQL SA → SQL Server with AHUB, No licenses → PostgreSQL 30-50% cheaper)
Ecosystem (Microsoft/.NET → SQL Server, Java/Python/Node.js → PostgreSQL)
Features (CLR/Service Broker → SQL Server, JSONB/PostGIS → PostgreSQL)
Scale (Single-node < 100GB → any, Massive scale > 100GB → PostgreSQL Hyperscale or SQL Hyperscale)
Team expertise (SQL Server DBAs → SQL Server initially, No database team → Managed PaaS either)
Cost (Budget constrained + no AHUB → PostgreSQL, Have AHUB → SQL Server competitive)

deployment_model_selection.title: Choosing Between PaaS and IaaS for Azure Databases
deployment_model_selection.description: Select appropriate deployment model balancing management overhead, control, compatibility, and cost
deployment_model_selection.rationale: PaaS (SQL Database, SQL MI, PostgreSQL Flexible) offers minimal management with automatic patching, backups, and HA. IaaS (SQL Server on VM, PostgreSQL on VM) provides full control with self-management. Most workloads benefit from PaaS, with IaaS reserved for specific compatibility or control requirements.

when_applicable: Choosing between managed service and self-managed
when_not_applicable: Organization policy mandates specific deployment model
Proof of concept or temporary workload (use quickest option)

options.name: PaaS - SQL Database
options.reason: Best for modern cloud-native applications with minimal management

pros: Automatic backups (7-35 days retention) and patching
cons: No SQL Server Agent (use Elastic Jobs)
No CLR assemblies or Service Broker
Higher cost than VMs without AHUB ($2,400 vs $1,200/month)

context_requirements: Don't need SQL Server Agent or cross-database queries

options.name: PaaS - SQL Managed Instance
options.reason: Best for lift-and-shift SQL Server migrations with near 100% compatibility

pros: SQL Server Agent, cross-database queries, CLR, Service Broker
cons: Higher cost than SQL Database ($2,800 vs $2,400/month for 8vCore)
Instance startup time (hours for initial provision)
Cannot change instance collation after creation

context_requirements: Need cross-database queries or SQL Server Agent
Want PaaS benefits with high compatibility

options.name: PaaS - PostgreSQL Flexible Server
options.reason: Best for PostgreSQL workloads with fully managed service

pros: Lower cost than SQL Server ($1,200 vs $2,400/month)
cons: Cannot modify postgresql.conf directly (use server parameters)

options.name: IaaS - SQL Server on Azure VM
options.reason: Best when need full SQL Server control or specific requirements

pros: Full OS and SQL Server control
Lower compute cost ($800 vs $2,400/month without AHUB)
Azure Hybrid Benefit saves up to 55%
cons: Self-managed HA (configure Always On AG or FCI)
Need database and OS administration expertise

context_requirements: Need specific SQL Server version or full features
Third-party applications with SQL Server dependencies
Team has SQL Server + Windows administration expertise

options.name: IaaS - PostgreSQL on Azure VM
options.reason: Only when need superuser access or specific PostgreSQL configuration

cons: No built-in PgBouncer or managed read replicas

context_requirements: Specific PostgreSQL version or extension not in Flexible Server
Team has PostgreSQL + Linux administration expertise

decision_factors: Management overhead (Want minimal → PaaS, Need full control → IaaS)
Compatibility (SQL Server features needed → SQL MI or VM, PostgreSQL standard → Flexible Server)
Expertise (Limited database team → PaaS, Experienced DBAs → can use IaaS)
Deployment speed (Fast → PaaS minutes, Custom → IaaS hours)
Cost with AHUB (Have SA → SQL VM competitive, No SA → PostgreSQL Flexible Server cheaper)
Features needed (SQL Agent/cross-DB → SQL MI, Superuser → VM, Standard features → PaaS)
